questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening pain in both knees over the past year. The pain is worse after prolonged sitting and improves with movement, but is aggravated by walking up stairs. She has no morning stiffness lasting more than 10 minutes. On examination, there is crepitus on knee flexion and bony enlargement around the joint line. Her inflammatory markers are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Osteoarthritis (OA) is suggested by gradual onset, activity-related pain, brief morning stiffness, crepitus, bony swelling, and normal inflammatory markers. Rheumatoid arthritis would have prolonged morning stiffness and systemic inflammation. Gout/pseudogout cause acute severe attacks. Patellofemoral pain affects younger patients without bony changes.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old retired mechanic complains of right hip pain that radiates to his groin and inner thigh. He finds it difficult to put on his socks and shoes. Symptoms are worse on walking and improve with rest. On exam, internal rotation of the hip is restricted and painful. His lumbar spine exam is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
questions.explanation: Groin-based pain with restricted internal rotation is classic for hip osteoarthritis. Lumbar radiculopathy typically radiates below the knee. Trochanteric bursitis has lateral hip tenderness. Impingement is more common in younger adults. AVN often has risk factors and more acute onset.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with pain at the base of her right thumb that has been progressive over several months. She notes difficulty gripping objects and pinching. On examination, there is tenderness at the first carpometacarpal joint and a 'square' appearance of the hand. Grind test reproduces pain. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Osteoarthritis of the first carpometacarpal joint
questions.explanation: Pain at thumb base with square hand deformity and positive grind test is characteristic of CMC joint OA. De Quervain's has Finkelstein test pain. Carpal tunnel causes nocturnal hand numbness. Scaphoid fracture has snuffbox tenderness after trauma. Trigger thumb has locking/nodule.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with known mild knee osteoarthritis presents with a two-week history of pain and swelling in the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints of both hands. The joints are red, warm, and tender. He has Heberden's nodes on his distal interphalangeal joints. Blood tests show normal urate and mild elevation of ESR (30 mm/h). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Erosive osteoarthritis presents with acute inflammatory episodes in PIP/DIP joints with Heberden's nodes, and spares metacarpophalangeal joints (unlike RA). Psoriatic arthritis usually has skin/nail changes. Gout/pseudogout show crystal-induced inflammation; here inflammatory markers are only mildly raised and urate normal.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman complains of leg pain and heaviness when walking that is relieved by sitting or leaning forward. She has no pain at rest. She has a history of back stiffness in the morning that goes away. On exam, lumbar extension is limited, and there is no lower limb swelling or vascular insufficiency. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Neurogenic claudication from spinal stenosis (often due to OA of lumbar spine) presents with leg pain on walking that eases with forward flexion (sitting). Vascular claudication has absent pulses and relief on standing still. Disc herniation is more acute with radicular pain. Hip OA has groin pain. Cauda equina causes saddle anaesthesia/bowel/bladder symptoms.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with bilateral knee pain that is worse with walking and improves with rest. She has no morning stiffness. On examination, there is crepitus and bony enlargement but no warmth or effusion. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of both knees
questions.explanation: Plain radiograph is the first-line imaging to confirm OA by showing osteophytes, joint space narrowing, or subchondral sclerosis. Anti-CCP and ESR are for inflammatory arthritis, not OA. MRI is reserved for atypical presentations or complications.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with pain and stiffness in the small joints of his hands, especially the distal interphalangeal joints. He has noticed bony swellings at the DIP joints. Morning stiffness lasts less than 30 minutes. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Clinical features of Heberden's nodes and typical distribution suggest OA. Plain radiograph will show osteophytes, joint space narrowing, and subchondral sclerosis, confirming OA. Rheumatoid factor and ANA are for RA/SLE; ultrasound may show osteophytes but X-ray is standard; DXA is for bone density.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known knee osteoarthritis presents to the Emergency Department with acute onset of a hot, swollen, and painful knee. She is unable to weight bear. Her temperature is 38.2°C. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to guide management?
options: Aspiration of the knee joint for Gram stain and culture
questions.explanation: In acute monoarthritis with fever and inability to weight bear, septic arthritis must be excluded. Joint aspiration for Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis is the gold standard and should be performed urgently. Blood tests (CRP, blood cultures) are supportive but not diagnostic. Imaging (X-ray, MRI) may show OA changes but cannot rule out infection.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known hip osteoarthritis reports a sudden increase in pain over the past week, now present at rest and waking him at night. He has no history of trauma. His hip X-ray from six months ago showed moderate OA changes. What is the most appropriate investigation to assess for a complication of his condition?
options: Repeat plain radiograph of the hip
questions.explanation: New severe rest pain and night pain raise suspicion for avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head. MRI is the most sensitive and specific imaging for early AVN, detecting bone marrow oedema before structural collapse. X-ray may be normal early; CT shows late changes; bone scan is less specific.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease stage 3 presents with gradual-onset bilateral knee pain that is worse on activity. She has no significant morning stiffness. On examination, there is crepitus and mild bony swelling. According to NICE guidelines, what is the most appropriate method to confirm the diagnosis of osteoarthritis in this patient?
options: Serum inflammatory markers and rheumatoid factor
Clinical diagnosis based on symptoms and signs alone
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend that OA can be diagnosed clinically without imaging in patients over 45 years with activity-related joint pain and no morning stiffness >30 minutes. Imaging is reserved for atypical presentations. This patient fits the typical profile, so a clinical diagnosis is most appropriate.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with first episode of mild right knee pain on walking. Examination shows no effusion, slight crepitus. X-ray shows minor joint space narrowing. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends topical NSAIDs as first-line for knee OA; they are more effective than paracetamol and avoid systemic side effects. Oral NSAIDs are second-line due to GI risk, and hyaluronic acid is not routinely recommended.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with bilateral hand osteoarthritis complains of joint stiffness and pain interfering with daily activities. He has no contraindications. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?
questions.explanation: For hand OA, topical NSAIDs are first-line due to efficacy and safety; paracetamol is less effective, oral NSAIDs carry GI risk, capsaicin is second-line, and glucosamine is not recommended by NICE.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known bilateral hip OA presents with an acute exacerbation of left hip pain. She is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation. Examination shows painful restricted movement. X-ray confirms severe OA. Which treatment is most likely contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen) increase bleeding risk when combined with warfarin and are contraindicated. Paracetamol, codeine, topical NSAIDs, and intra-articular steroids are safe with appropriate monitoring.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known knee OA presents with a tense effusion and severe pain. He has no contraindications to steroids. What is the most appropriate first-line intervention?
questions.explanation: For acute flare with effusion, intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides rapid relief; aspiration alone is less effective and usually combined with injection. Physiotherapy and oral analgesics are not first-line for acute severe effusion.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old patient with knee OA and stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 35 mL/min) presents with mild pain. What is the most appropriate first-line analgesic?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs are contraindicated in CKD due to risk of worsening renal function; paracetamol is safe and first-line. Codeine may be considered if paracetamol inadequate but carries risk of constipation and falls in elderly.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to ED with acute-onset severe pain, swelling, and redness of her left knee. She has known bilateral knee osteoarthritis (OA) managed with paracetamol and ibuprofen as needed. She is unable to weight-bear. Temperature is 38.2°C, HR 98, BP 130/80. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give a bolus of intravenous morphine
Apply a compression bandage and arrange for physiotherapy
Aspirate the knee joint for synovial fluid analysis
questions.explanation: Acute monoarthritis with fever and inability to weight-bear is septic arthritis until proven otherwise. Immediate joint aspiration is diagnostic (synovial fluid culture, Gram stain, crystal analysis) and also therapeutic. Starting antibiotics without aspiration would compromise culture yield. X-ray and pain relief are secondary; physiotherapy is inappropriate in acute septic arthritis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with bilateral knee OA for 5 years attends GP. Pain is limiting walking to 200 metres. He has tried regular paracetamol and ibuprofen gel with partial relief. On examination, body mass index (BMI) is 34 kg/m², knee crepitus and mild effusion are present. He asks about the best next step. What is the single most appropriate management?
options: Advise weight loss and refer for a supervised exercise programme
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines emphasise core treatments: weight loss (BMI >25) and exercise (land-based or water-based) as first-line for OA. The patient has not yet tried these. Referral for arthroplasty is premature without optimising conservative measures. Opioids are not recommended long-term. MRI and corticosteroids are not routine for OA at this stage.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with hand OA affecting her distal interphalangeal joints complains of persistent pain and stiffness despite using paracetamol and topical NSAIDs. She has a past history of peptic ulcer disease. What is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?
options: Refer to pain clinic for opioid therapy
Administer intra-articular corticosteroid injection for hand OA
Start oral ibuprofen with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI)
questions.explanation: In patients with contraindications to NSAIDs (previous peptic ulcer), NICE recommends considering low-dose amitriptyline for chronic pain in OA. Oral NSAIDs with PPI are an option but should be used cautiously; naproxen without PPI is unsafe. Intra-articular injections are not routine for hand joints. Opioids are third-line.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with known hip OA is admitted to the ward with confusion and reduced mobility. He is in bed, unable to stand. Nursing staff note the right leg is shortened and externally rotated. He has severe pain on any attempt to move the right hip. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give oral analgesia and request a physiotherapy assessment
Request routine blood tests and a CT head
Apply gentle traction and offer a commode
questions.explanation: The history and examination (shortened, externally rotated leg) suggest a fractured neck of femur, a common complication of OA in elderly with falls. Immediate X-ray is needed to confirm and plan urgent orthopaedic management. Analgesia should be given but not before diagnosis; physiotherapy and traction are premature before confirming fracture. CT head is not indicated without neurological signs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with unilateral hip OA is reviewed. She has moderate pain that limits gardening. She has tried paracetamol and exercise. She also has hypertension well-controlled on ramipril. She has not had any gastrointestinal issues. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange an ultrasound-guided intra-articular hip injection
questions.explanation: NICE recommends oral NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) as second-line after paracetamol and exercise, provided no contraindications. This patient has controlled hypertension and no GI issues, so a standard NSAID is appropriate. Codeine is less preferred due to side effects. Topical capsaicin is for hand/knee OA. Arthroplasty is for refractory severe pain. Intra-articular injections may be considered but are not first-line pharmacological step after simple analgesia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known bilateral knee osteoarthritis presents with a 2-day history of acute right knee pain, swelling, and inability to weight bear. Her temperature is 38.2°C. Which clinical finding is most concerning for a life-threatening mimic of an OA flare?
questions.explanation: Fever in the context of an acutely swollen, painful knee is a red flag for septic arthritis, which is a medical emergency. OA flares typically do not cause systemic symptoms; a cool joint is the norm.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with known hip osteoarthritis presents to the Emergency Department after a minor fall. He has severe right hip pain and cannot bear weight. Which physical finding most strongly suggests a fracture rather than an OA flare?
questions.explanation: Leg shortening and external rotation are classic signs of a displaced neck of femur fracture. OA flares cause pain and stiffness but not deformity; groin pain and stiffness are less specific.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with long-standing lumbar spondylosis (spinal osteoarthritis) presents with worsening lower back pain and bilateral leg numbness. Which examination finding is a red flag for cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: Saddle anaesthesia is a key red flag for cauda equina syndrome, which requires urgent spinal decompression. OA alone does not cause sensory loss in a saddle distribution.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known lumbar osteoarthritis presents with a 3-day history of severe lower back pain and fever (38.5°C). Which examination finding most strongly suggests a spinal infection rather than an OA flare?
options: Percussion tenderness over a single spinous process
questions.explanation: Percussion tenderness localised to a single spinous process raises suspicion for vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis, which is a life-threatening infection. OA causes diffuse tenderness and no fever.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with knee osteoarthritis is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation. He presents with acute severe right knee pain and swelling that started spontaneously. On examination, the knee is tense, warm, and there is extensive bruising around the joint. Which finding is most concerning?
questions.explanation: Extensive ecchymosis in the setting of an acute tense effusion suggests haemarthrosis, which can lead to compartment syndrome and is a complication of anticoagulation. OA flares do not cause bruising.
